data.title: Hong Kong passport ranks 15th globally, rising 3 places in visa-free access index
data.description:
Hongkongers hold the world’s 15th most powerful passport, up three places from last year, according to an index measuring visa-free access.
The Hong Kong Special Administrative Region passport offers visa-free access to 171 destinations, according to this year’s Henley Passport Index released last week. Singapore retained the top ranking for the third consecutive year, granting its citizens visa-free entry to 192 destinations.
However, data from the Hong Kong Immigration Department shows that 174 countries and territories currently grant visa-free or visa-on-arrival access to holders of the city’s passport, which remains valid for travel to all countries worldwide.
Another passport commonly held by Hongkongers born before the 1997 handover – the British National (Overseas) or BN(O) passport – is not included in the index.
The Hong Kong passport also trails behind Japan and South Korea in Asia, which share second place in the world rankings with visa-free access to 188 destinations, “reinforcing Asia’s long-standing leadership at the top of the global mobility rankings”, said the London-headquartered firm Henley & Partners, which specialises in residence and citizenship planning.
The index is based on data from the International Air Transport Association and compiled by Henley & Partners’ research team.

Denmark, Luxembourg, Spain, Sweden and Switzerland share third place, each offering visa-free access to 186 destinations, while an unprecedented group of 10 European countries – Austria, Belgium, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Italy, the Netherlands and Norway – tied for fourth.
According to the index, the remainder of the top 10 “underscored Europe’s dominance”, with exceptions including the United Arab Emirates in fifth place, New Zealand in sixth, Australia in seventh, Canada in eighth, and Malaysia in ninth.
Iraq, Syria and Afghanistan were ranked lowest, from 99th to 101st place, with visa-free access to 29, 26 and 24 destinations respectively.
Marking its 20th anniversary, the index also revealed insights into its rankings, noting that while the US and the UK remain in the top 10, there has been a “longer-term decline” for both countries since they jointly held first place in 2014.
Both recorded their sharpest annual declines in visa-free access last year, losing seven and eight destinations respectively, while the UAE stood out as the strongest performer, adding 140 visa-free destinations over the past two decades.

data.title: Tesla’s defeat, Harvard’s success, Trump ‘top global risk’: 7 US-China relations reads
data.description:
We have selected seven of the most interesting and important news stories covering US-China relations from the past few weeks. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. BYD’s win, Tesla’s defeat, Ford’s pivot: the widening gap in US-China EV markets

After years of competing head-to-head with China in electric vehicles, the United States is now widely seen as falling behind – a reality increasingly acknowledged even within Detroit.
Read the full story here.
2. ‘Shocked’ China demands immediate release of Nicolas Maduro

China delivered a strongly worded message to the United States at an “emergency” meeting of the UN Security Council on January 6, calling on Washington to abide by international law, end its illusion that it is the world’s police force and court, and immediately release ex-Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro and his wife.
Read the full story here.
3. US poet laureate Arthur Sze on slowing down to translate poetry across cultures

When Arthur Sze translates classical Tang dynasty poetry into English, he writes Chinese characters by hand, slowing himself to the pace of brush strokes and gaps. This deliberate resistance to speed is central to the craft of the US’ 25th poet laureate, and reflective of his belief that poetry must be treated with care if it is to cross borders with depth intact.
Read the full story here.
4. Chinese student numbers at Harvard rise despite Trump visa crackdown

Enrolment at Harvard for Chinese students rose in the autumn of 2025 from a year earlier, even as the Donald Trump administration moved to rein in visas for them and limit foreign enrolment and funding at the prestigious university.
Read the full story here.
5. China, Russia won’t ‘dare test’ US, says Hegseth after Venezuela military op

US Secretary of Defence Pete Hegseth delivered a stark warning to China and Russia in January, declaring that the United States is rebuilding military dominance to a level so absolute that its enemies would “not dare” test it.
Read the full story here.
6. Trump seen as top global risk in 2026 as US slips further behind China

The US is in danger of falling increasingly behind China in 2026 as the Asian giant tightens its grip on drones, battery storage, robots and manufacturing, although the No 1 risk to global stability is the US under President Donald Trump, Eurasia Group said on January 5 in its closely watched annual forecast of threats, challenges and hotspots.
Read the full story here.
7. California progressive to lead Democrats on influential House China panel

It was announced that Ro Khanna, a California progressive who opposed the US congressional effort to force the Chinese divestment of TikTok, will replace Illinois Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi as the top Democrat on the House Select Committee on the Communist Party later this month.
Read the full story here.

data.title: China’s charm offensive: Beijing bypasses Brussels in bid to soften EU trade push
data.description:
Hua Chunying, China’s foreign vice-minister, beamed this week as she presented a leading Czech lawmaker with a decorative porcelain plate – a gift that signalled a possible thaw in ties between Prague and Beijing.
The image sent tongues wagging in Europe: a symbol that a new Chinese charm offensive towards a Europe jilted by its erstwhile ally across the Atlantic had reached even its most hawkish capitals.
China has spent the opening weeks of the year courting European governments, offering market access, visas and symbolic gestures to selected EU member states, even as relations with Brussels continue to sour.
From aircraft orders in France to reopened beef and pork markets for Ireland and Spain, Beijing is leaning hard into bilateral diplomacy as the European Commission prepares for another confrontational year of trade action against China.


Several sources said Chinese diplomats were using these channels to disparage policies cooked up in Brussels and sow disunity among the 27-member bloc, which in recent years has pursued an increasingly tough trade and geopolitical approach to the world’s second-largest economy.
According to a senior EU source speaking on condition of anonymity, the charm offensive was “in full swing … they don’t like us here in Brussels but know they can get much more from the member states”.
While there is openness in Brussels to improving ties with Beijing in light of the geopolitical upheaval that has defined the start of 2026, it would depend on major changes in China’s trade policies and its relations with Russia.
Officials here are well accustomed to playing the bad cop to the good cop national governments, who prefer to let Brussels do the dirty work on trade gripes and human rights while cutting deals to boost their own economies. Already on this front, this year appears set for business as usual.
A huge pipeline of trade and competition investigations is in the works as Brussels looks to tackle a gaping EU-wide trade deficit with China that skyrocketed 18.1 per cent last year, Chinese customs data published this week showed. In Germany alone, the deficit more than doubled, up 108 per cent.
Officials are not fixated on the raw numbers but are concerned about what they believe it represents: the suspected export of subsidised, cut-price industrial overcapacity that threatens Europe’s manufacturing future.
With that in mind, a foreign subsidies regulation (FSR) investigation into wind turbines is expected to be formally announced soon, and in the rail industry, rolling stock is also in the crosshairs of those regulators.
The FSR, a fast-moving competition tool that requires investigated firms to hand over copious sensitive information at breakneck speed, is tipped to be highly active this year.
For years, EU officials have griped about “divide and rule” tactics employed by major powers towards Europe. By courting individual members, both the United States and China have been able to dull the sharpest edges of EU policies and dilute ambitious strategies.
Aware of the short shrift it was likely to receive in Brussels, Beijing had seized the start of a new year to accelerate their efforts, the sources said. With the US in the process of dismantling the global order it helped to build, China is likely to find some receptive interlocutors.

For fiscally strained France, the acquisition of 148 Airbus jets could be worth billions of euros. However, the timing raised eyebrows in Brussels: the deals were publicised by Chinese state media as European regulators arrived in Shanghai to begin flight testing Beijing’s own C919 jet – a step to ensuring it attains Western accreditation.
During a recent state visit to Beijing, Irish Taoiseach Micheal Martin was given the red-carpet treatment, face time with Chinese President Xi Jinping and a guided tour of the Forbidden City.
But Martin will have been most pleased with the reopening of the Chinese beef market to Irish exports following a 2024 ban due to the discovery of a case of mad cow disease.
Spanish pork exports to China resumed in December, despite an outbreak of African swine fever in the country, causing upset in Brussels that market access was restored on a bilateral basis rather than EU-wide under the bloc’s common sanitary and trade rules.
Furthermore, as fractures grow between the EU and the US, Beijing is presenting itself as a steady trade partner and floating a revival of a long-stalled investment pact.
In a call with Finnish Foreign Minister Elina Valtonen on January 6, her Chinese counterpart, Wang Yi, even suggested the two sides “actively explore the negotiation and signing of a China-EU free trade agreement”.
Perhaps most revealing of Beijing’s goodwill drive, however, were images of the delighted Hua in Prague, where she met senior lawmakers and the Czech foreign vice-minister. Czechia had in recent years pursued the EU’s most pro-Taiwan strategy and been a vocal critic of mainland China.
With a new populist government pledging to revamp foreign relations, however, Hua discussed visa-free access to China for Czech nationals and restarting direct flights to Shanghai, according to a post by Radek Vondráček, the head of the parliament’s foreign policy committee.
By contrast, officials noted that when China’s Ministry of Commerce announced tariffs on EU dairy exports over the holiday period, only one company was designated the top end 42.7 per cent rate: FrieslandCampina of the Netherlands, which has been embroiled in a dispute with Beijing over the chipmaker Nexperia. The message interpreted in Brussels: it pays for capitals to remain in Beijing’s good graces.


Meanwhile, between Brussels and Chinese trade officials, there are open channels but little good news to toast.
An EU-China summit is unlikely to happen in the first half of 2026, and some officials doubt whether one will happen this year at all. According to well-placed sources, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and European Council President Antonio Costa will certainly not travel to Beijing for a third successive summit there.
French President Emmanuel Macron is considering inviting Xi to the Group of Seven summit he will host this summer, meaning the EU-China meeting could theoretically be bolted onto that. Brussels officials are sceptical, however, whether Xi would accept.
American officials have pushed back strongly against the idea, even if it is thought that US President Donald Trump would be amenable. The main obstacle could be the presence of Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, whose comments on Taiwan in November drew a sharp and extensive trade and military response from Beijing.
On electric vehicles, EU officials downplayed a decision to publish guidelines outlining how Chinese EV makers could eliminate anti-subsidy duties by agreeing minimum prices for their cars, despite it being trumpeted as a major deal by Beijing. A pact was far from in sight, they said.
“We are not ‘weighing minimum prices to replace the duties’ countervailing Chinese subsidies,” wrote Sabine Weyand, the EU’s director general for trade, in a social media post clarifying that a deal to end the EV dispute had yet to be reached. “It is normal practice under anti-dumping/anti-subsidy rules that individual companies can offer price undertakings.”
“We have had one company offering so, and we have now published guidance in case any other companies would envisage that,” Weyand added. “In any case, such price undertakings have to be equally effective as the countervailing duties.”
Before Christmas, EU trade ministers pledged to stay united and avoid being picked apart by Beijing’s overtures. But as Brussels readies a wave of enforcement actions, China is already testing whether national political incentives will blunt the bloc’s collective resolve.

data.title: Hongkonger reported missing in Iran ‘for many days’ amid internet shutdown, protests
data.description:
Immigration authorities have confirmed receiving a plea for help concerning a Hong Kong resident reported missing “for many days” in Iran, as the country faces a nationwide internet shutdown amid protests against the government.
A spokesman for the Immigration Department told the Post on Friday that it had received “a relevant inquiry” and was in touch with the Chinese consulate in Bandar Abbas and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs’ local arm in the city.
A source confirmed the inquiry came from the family of a young female traveller who could not be contacted from Iran.
A Threads post by a travel blogger known as “Bing” gained traction on Thursday, where a user named “hmting” commented below that Bing was her sister and had gone missing.
The post, published on January 6, featured a short compilation video of Bing’s journey in Iran, showing film photos and a collection of short clips of the female traveller and people she had met.
“[A] Hong Kong passport can only get visa-free [entry] for 21 days, I will dash to the passport office tomorrow to extend one month’s visa,” Bing wrote on the post.
This was her most recent post on both her Threads and Instagram accounts.
The comment from the user identifying herself as Bing’s sister was published on Thursday and has gained nearly 200 comments as other users inquired about the blogger’s safety and tried to raise attention to the matter.
“My younger sister is now in Iran and has been missing for many days. I’ve found the Immigration Department for help,” the user said.

Hmting continued in her comment to ask if there were any other means to reach her sister, adding another query for anyone who knew people staying in “BB Hostel”.
Without disclosing any details, the Immigration Department said it would continue to maintain close liaison with the Commissioner’s Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the consulate and family members.
The department added it would proactively follow up on the matter and provide appropriate help in accordance with the family’s wishes.
Iran has imposed a nationwide internet shutdown since last Thursday amid growing protests across the country against the ruling Islamic regime.
Human rights organisations had warned that Iranian authorities would launch violent crackdowns against protesters amid the internet blackout.
US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency reported that at least 640 people had been killed as of Monday since protests broke out on December 28 last year. An Iranian official told Reuters that around 2,000 people were killed, blaming “terrorists” for the deaths of civilians and security personnel.

data.title: New Indonesia head coach John Herdman calls on team to turn disappointment into success
data.description:
New Indonesia head coach John Herdman has called on the country to harness the disappointment of previous failures to fuel ‌qualification for a first World Cup and to challenge for regional and continental titles.
Herdman ‍was officially unveiled as Patrick Kluivert’s successor on Monday after the Dutchman parted company with the Indonesians following an unsuccessful attempt to qualify for the 2026 World Cup finals.
Indonesia reached the fourth round of Asia’s qualifiers for the first time ⁠last year with a squad built largely from players born overseas, and Herdman said that the experience will provide a platform for the team to improve.
“I’m sure the fans want to be at the World Cup in 2026 but you have to suffer, you have to feel it,” Herdman said following the draw for ‍the group stages of the Asean Championship on Thursday.
“This was a new group of players, Indonesian players ‍trying to come together and connect in a short period of time.

“The talent’s there but often that talent takes time to come together, to really feel what you ‌feel. Those defeats are what create the foundation for the future.
“That group of men, we have no excuses ‍now. We’ve suffered together, the talent’s there and we have the opportunity to take this country to the next level.”
Herdman’s first games in charge will come in March in the Fifa Series, which will be played in Jakarta, before the country attempts to win the ‌Asean Championship ⁠for the first time in the competition’s 30-year history in July and August.
The 50-year-old Englishman, who led Canada to the 2022 World Cup, will then lead Indonesia towards the Asian Cup finals in Saudi Arabia in January 2027, with the nation looking to build on its best run to the last ‌16 in the previous edition in Qatar.
“That pressure is an absolute privilege for the players, it’s a curse as well if we want to view it like that,” Herdman said of ‌the expectation placed upon his ‌players by the country’s football-mad population of 280 million people.
“But the mentality we’re building is a pioneering mindset. We haven’t qualified for the World Cup, we haven’t won an Asian Cup, we haven’t won an Asean Hyundai Cup. We have a chance to do all of those things. That’s the mindset. That’s the privilege.”

data.title: UK teen who praised Southport murderer jailed for possessing al-Qaeda manual
data.description:
A British teenager who praised the killer of three young girls at a Taylor Swift-themed dance event ‌and said he planned to bomb British rock band Oasis’ reunion concert ‍was sentenced to detention on Friday for possession of an al-Qaeda manual.
McKenzie Morgan, 18, was arrested at his home in Wales after sending messages on the social media platform Snapchat in ⁠which he praised Axel Rudakubana, who murdered three girls and stabbed 10 others in July 2024, prosecutor Corinne Bramwell said.
Morgan told a psychiatric nurse on the morning of his arrest in June that he “planned to commit a Rudakubana-style terrorist attack” and had been ‍researching how to stab people, Bramwell told Morgan’s sentencing hearing at London’s Old Bailey court.

The ‍teenager twice tried to buy a 15cm (6-inch) kitchen knife from Amazon, searched online for local playgrounds and a youth dance academy and ‌put the academy on a document on his mobile phone entitled “places to attack”, Bramwell added.
She ‍said Morgan later told another Snapchat user that he planned to bomb the Oasis concert in Cardiff on July 4, 2025, the band’s first gig of their comeback tour, and claimed to have tried to make ‌the deadly ⁠poison ricin.

He was arrested on June 2, and a 188-page al-Qaeda training manual was found on one of his electronic devices. Morgan pleaded guilty to a single count of possession of information likely to be of use to a person ‌engaged in terrorism.
Morgan accepted having saved the al-Qaeda manual and reading it, but told police he had no intention to commit an attack ‌and simply intended to ‌shock others with his messages.
He has been diagnosed with autism and two psychiatrists assessed Morgan as being vulnerable to being groomed or radicalised online, Bramwell told the court.
Judge Sarah Whitehouse sentenced Morgan to 14 months’ detention in a young offenders institution.

data.title: AI is reshaping US-China tech race – can electricity tilt the balance?
data.description:
China’s state-owned power grid giant has pledged to increase fixed-asset investment by 40 per cent over the next five years, at a time when some analysts view electricity capacity as a strategic asset in the US-China rivalry amid soaring demand from artificial intelligence (AI) and other power-hungry industries.
The State Grid Corporation of China expects the investment amount to reach 4 trillion yuan (US$574 billion) through 2030, a company record, as it accelerates efforts to build a “new-type power system” – a more efficient and flexible grid designed to integrate renewables.
The expansion could raise electricity’s share of end-use energy consumption to 35 per cent, with about 200 gigawatts of wind and solar capacity added annually, the company announced on Thursday.
The massive investment comes as the world grapples with the enormous energy appetite of AI. The International Energy Agency estimated that from 2024 to 2030, China’s data centres would consume 170 per cent more electricity while the consumption in the US would increase 130 per cent, with both accounting for nearly 80 per cent of global growth.
However, China and the US – the world’s two AI powerhouses – face completely different pictures. China tops all countries with a third of global electricity generation volume due to aggressive government planning, while the US is facing an intensifying power shortage, prompting tech giants from Google to Meta to acquire power assets to secure supply for training their AI models.
“In the AI race, computing power comes down to chips, electricity and algorithms – and electricity is the one area where China holds an undeniable advantage,” said Peng Peng, executive chairman of the Guangdong Society of Reform, a think tank affiliated with the provincial government.


Pierre Lau Hin-tat, China equity strategist at Citigroup Global Markets, said State Grid’s investment had “beat market expectations” and would help China “cement its edge over the United States in power supply”.
Shares of Chinese electricity and grid equipment makers surged on Friday morning after State Grid unveiled the 4 trillion yuan (US$574 billion) plan to upgrade the country’s power networks.
Tesla and SpaceX CEO Elon Musk has also predicted that China will lead the world in AI computing power, estimating that its electricity output – already the world’s largest – could reach about three times that of the US’ by 2026, giving it the capacity to support energy-hungry data centres.
“China’s going to have more power than anyone else and probably will have more chips,” he told the “Moonshots with Peter Diamandis” podcast, in an episode released on January 6.
China’s total electricity generation was expected to reach about 10.6 trillion kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025, according to the National Energy Administration. By comparison, US power plants were projected to generate about 4.24 trillion kWh in the same year, according to the US Energy Information Administration – suggesting that China may have produced more than twice as much.
The State Grid Corporation of China said it also plans to fast-track ultra-high-voltage transmission projects, boosting cross-regional and cross-provincial power transfer capacity by more than 30 per cent by the end of the current five-year plan. It also aims to further electrify end-use energy demand, including connecting 35 million electric vehicle charging facilities to the grid.
The company said the expansion would help raise the share of non-fossil fuels in China’s energy mix to 25 per cent, advancing the national goals of building a new energy system and achieving economy-wide carbon peaking on schedule.

data.title: Taipei hails US 15% tariffs ‘home run’ despite fears over US$500 billion cost
data.description:
Taiwan has secured a US deal slashing export tariffs to 15 per cent after more than nine months of talks, with Taipei hailing the result as a “home run” that would put the island on an equal footing with Japan and South Korea.
But observers and opposition parties have raised sharp concerns over Taiwan’s commitment to invest up to US$500 billion in the United States in exchange for the tariff cut.
The arrangement could hollow out Taiwan’s industrial base – particularly its prized semiconductor sector – while delivering disproportionate gains for Washington, they warned.
Beijing has vehemently objected to the deal. “The Chinese side has consistently and firmly opposed countries that have established diplomatic relations with China entering into any agreements with the Taiwan region,” Guo Jiakun, a spokesman for the mainland foreign ministry, said on Friday.
On Wednesday, Beijing’s Taiwan Affairs Office spokeswoman Zhu Fenglian described the expected deal as “economic exploitation” of the island and a plot to drain the lifeblood of its industry.
She also condemned Taiwan’s ruling Democratic Progressive Party for bowing to US pressure. “[This] will only destroy the development prospect of Taiwan and harm the long-term interests of the Taiwanese people,” she cautioned.
Beijing views Taiwan as a part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
The US agreement, announced on Thursday, lowers tariff rates from 20 per cent to 15 per cent, aligning Taiwan with other major American trade partners, including Japan, South Korea and the European Union.
Taiwanese officials have stated that the deal also secures preferential treatment under potential future US Section 232 tariffs on semiconductors and other sensitive products.
Taiwan’s Vice-Premier Cheng Li‑chun, who led the talks, said in a news conference aired live from the island’s representative office in Washington on Friday that the negotiations achieved “four core objectives”.
These included tariff parity, preferential treatment for semiconductors and semiconductor derivatives, and what she described as a uniquely flexible investment framework dubbed the “Taiwan model”.
Under that model, Taiwanese companies would plan and execute up to US$250 billion in direct investment in the US, while the Taiwanese government would provide credit guarantees enabling financial institutions to extend up to another US$250 billion in financing.
Cheng stressed that the guarantees did not constitute direct government spending and that companies would retain autonomy over investment decisions.
“This is not industrial relocation, but the extension and expansion of Taiwan’s hi-tech industries,” Cheng said, rejecting claims that the deal amounted to hollowing out Taiwan’s economy.
She added that the US had committed to helping Taiwanese firms with land access, utilities, infrastructure, tax incentives and visa programmes to help build industrial clusters.
Taiwanese leader William Lai Ching‑te echoed that message, saying on Friday that the agreement would strengthen Taiwan’s global competitiveness and deepen hi-tech cooperation with Washington.
Premier Cho Jung-tai went further, calling the outcome a “beautiful home run” that showed Washington viewed Taiwan as a strategic partner.
As long as the main peak of Taiwan’s “sacred mountain” – a common metaphor for its semiconductor industry – remained at home, he said, the island could “stand firm domestically while deploying globally”.
But remarks by US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick have fuelled controversy in Taiwan.
Lutnick told American business news outlet CNBC in an interview soon after the talks that the goal under President Donald Trump was to “bring 40 per cent of Taiwan’s entire [semiconductor] supply chain and production” capacity to the US.
Firms failing to build plants in the US could face tariffs as high as 100 per cent, he warned.
This has prompted alarm among Taiwan’s opposition parties, who argue that the deal reflects unequal bargaining power rather than a partnership of equals.
The main opposition Kuomintang (KMT) – a Beijing-friendly party – accused the Lai government of “dressing up a damaging outcome as a triumph”.
KMT lawmakers said a “15 per cent tariff rate merely matches Japan and South Korea”, calling it a baseline outcome rather than an achievement, while the investment commitments “represent an unprecedented burden relative to Taiwan’s economic size”.
Requiring as much as 40 per cent of Taiwan’s chip capacity to move overseas would strip the island of its core strategic assets, KMT chairwoman Cheng Li‑wun said.
“If investing in the US were genuinely attractive, companies would have gone without geopolitical pressure,” she said, accusing the DPP government of “selling off the family silver” to please Washington.
Other KMT lawmakers warned that the relocation would not stop with flagship firms such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSMC) but would pull entire upstream and downstream supply chains overseas, hitting small and medium-sized enterprises that lacked the resources to absorb losses.


The smaller opposition Taiwan People’s Party (TPP) voiced similar concerns, saying it supported closer Taiwan-US exchanges but opposed what it called “unilateral concessions made without sufficient democratic oversight”.
“Using public credit guarantees to back US investment could leave Taiwanese taxpayers exposed if projects fail,” a TPP statement warned.
Dachrahn Wu, a professor of economics at National Central University in Taoyuan, near Taipei, warned that diverting investment on such a scale to the US would inevitably crowd out Taiwan’s capital spending – a key driver of the island’s growth.
Once investment and talent moved, the impact would not be short-term, he said. “Over the next three to five years, economic growth, home consumption and even the housing market could be affected.”
Wu added that surging demand for US dollars to fund overseas investment could weaken the Taiwan dollar, benefiting exports but raising import prices and inflation risks.
Liu Da‑nien, director of the Regional Development Study Centre, Chung-Hua Institution for Economic Research, noted that while headline investment figures appeared similar to Japan and South Korea, Taiwan’s commitments were heavily concentrated in advanced chip manufacturing, in contrast to their more diversified portfolios. “That concentration deserves close scrutiny.”
Huang Kwei-bo, a professor of diplomacy at National Chengchi University in Taipei, warned that the US could weaken its supply-chain links with Taiwan in the event of a cross-strait conflict.
“Once advanced semiconductor supply chains are established in the US, Washington may push to move some production out of Taiwan in response to cross-strait risks,” he said.
“Together with talent and capital outflows, this would add significant uncertainty to Taiwan’s economic outlook.”

data.title: Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon: defending champ Kipkemboi eyes course record, title
data.description:
Reigning Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon champion Bethwell Kipkemboi is back in the city and determined not only to successfully defend his title on Sunday but also to break the course record.
Kipkemboi, who claimed last year’s men’s marathon crown in two hours, 11 minutes and 13 seconds, has set his sights on surpassing the 2:09:20 mark set by fellow Kenyan Barnabas Kiptum in 2019.
Even so, the 32-year-old said he did not feel any pressure and was “just focused” on his goal.
“I want to defend the title,” Kipkemboi said. “The course is tough, I know that, but it all depends on how you train before the race and I am ready to tackle it.”
He will need to overcome the challenge of Mekuant Ayenew as well as that posed by the course. The Ethiopian, who is ranked 98th in the world, is the fastest man in the field with a personal best of 2:04:46.

Among the women’s elite, Bahrain’s Shitaye Eshete, who was born in Ethiopia, will make her Hong Kong debut as the fastest of the invited female runners.
The three-time Asian champion, ranked 72nd in the world, said she was confident about handling the city’s demanding route, and believed she could “smash it”.
“I have heard Hong Kong’s course is very challenging with a lot of climbing in the second half,” the 35-year-old said. “Rutto [Kipkemboi] told me it is difficult.”
This year’s race will see a record number of invited overseas athletes, including five runners from North Korea among the 26 men and 19 women.
Kwan Kee, chairman of the race’s organising committee, said they had invested more in attracting elite competitors.
“Some top-level athletes even wrote to us asking to join. We’re very pleased with the response,” Kee said.
With several of the invited runners ranked among the world’s top 100, Kwan hoped some of Hong Kong’s best would be able to keep pace with some of them.
Wong Wan-chun, Hong Kong’s marathon record holder, is hoping to better the time of 2:21:04 he posted in being the fastest local runner last year. That time was 11 minutes faster than he managed when winning in 2024.
The 29-year-old said he would go into the run full of confidence after finishing this month’s Unicef Hero Run half-marathon around Hong Kong Disneyland Resort in 1:06:32.
Despite being Hong Kong’s fastest long-distance runner, Wong and the city’s athletics association, of which Kee is also the chairman, have been locked in a dispute that has stopped him competing in major overseas events.
“For the coming Asian Games in Nagoya [in September], I will just take things as they come,” Wong said. “I still hope the association can give me a clear and reasonable explanation for why the team Hong Kong application has not been processed.”
In the women’s local division, defending champion Virginia Lo Ying-chiu said the marathon remained the most important race of her year.
“For full-time athletes like me, it affects our livelihood, so we will give everything we have,” Lo said.
Lo, who was the eighth woman overall across the line last year, said the strong overseas field would push her to perform better.
“Running alongside top international runners will really motivate me and I hope their presence can drive me to go faster on Sunday,” she added.

data.title: Hong Kong’s pivot to hi-tech development gets boost as city hosts global AI summit
data.description:
Hong Kong took another big step in its journey to become an international tech hub on Friday, as the city basked in its inaugural turn as host of a summit under the World Artificial Intelligence Conference (WAIC).
In his video address to the conference held in the Hong Kong Science Park, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu said: “We must harness the power of AI, not just to innovate, but to build – to build more inclusive economies, more resilient communities and a more sustainable future for all.”
“The Recommendations of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China for formulating the 15th five-year plan for National Economic and Social Development supports Hong Kong’s development into an international innovation and technology centre, as well as an international hub for high-calibre talent.”
“Central to this vision is our commitment to advancing the development of AI,” Lee said, as he pointed out the city’s world-class research, strategic infrastructure and dynamic open market.
Hong Kong’s turn to host an event under WAIC, the main conference held annually in Shanghai since 2018, reflected Beijing’s confidence in the city’s strategic development goals.
“Today’s conference is not only a milestone, but also a new starting point,” said Hendrick Sin, a Hong Kong deputy to the 14th National People’s Congress. He said at the event that the WAIC organisers had committed to hold the summit annually in Hong Kong.

The city’s Secretary for Innovation, Technology and Industry, Sun Dong, noted that all economies around the world recognised AI’s strategic importance.
“Under the unique advantages of ‘one country, two systems’, Hong Kong was also seizing this AI moment,” he said. “With a focus on ‘strengthening infrastructure and promoting the application-oriented approach’, Hong Kong is steadily advancing a series of AI initiatives.”
He said the Hong Kong government had funded the establishment of 16 research and development laboratories focused on AI and robotics.
The city last month inaugurated its side of the Hetao Shenzhen-Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone, which Lee described at the event as a “world-class hub for technological innovation that connects the mainland with the international community”.
That development would enable “AI projects to rapidly progress from fundamental research in Hong Kong, to commercialisation and large-scale application in the broader market”, Lee said.
The audience at Friday’s WAIC event also heard remarks from Piero Scaruffi, director of the Silicon Valley Artificial Intelligence Research Institute, and Steve Hoffman, CEO of Silicon Valley incubator Founders Space, as well as top local academics including Guo Yike, provost of the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology.
At the main WAIC conference in July last year, keynote speeches were delivered by Chinese premier Li Qiang as well as Geoffrey Hinton, widely known as the “Godfather of AI”, in his first visit to the mainland.
